revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:平季基]] {{Infobox person|gender={{ill|male|qid=Q6581097}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11482381]] Wikidata name: Taira no Suemoto [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:42, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Cite-sources-needed|date=December 2022}} '''Taira no Suemoto''' (平 季基) was a regional government official during the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].
== Origin == Taira no Suemoto's lineage is unclear due to limited historical records. He is believed to be a member of the [[Kanmu Taira clan]] (桓武平氏), specifically of the [[Hanjō branch]] (繁盛流) connected to the [[Daijō clan]] and a cadet branch of the [[Iza clan]] of [[:en:Hitachi Province|Hitachi Province]]. This lineage includes prominent ancestors like [[Taira no Tamekane]], noted for his contributions during the [[:en:Toi invasion|Toi invasion]]. However, other accounts suggest Suemoto descended from the [[Bandō Hachihei]] (坂東八平氏), specifically the seventh-generation progeny of [[:en:Taira no Yoshifumi|Taira no Yoshifumi]], or that he belonged to the [[Satsuma Taira clan]] established by [[Taira no Sadatoki]]. Some scholars, such as Tokushige Asakichi, theorize that Taira no Suemoto was closely related to the descendants of [[Taira no Masasuke]] and [[Taira no Masayasu]], notable figures from the lineage of [[Taira no Takamune]]. What is certain is his role in the foundation and administration of [[Shima-tsu Manor]], one of Japan's largest estates.
=== Descendants === Suemoto's known descendants include [[Iza no Heiji Kanesuke]], who settled in [[:en:Hizen Province|Hizen Province]] and established the [[Kanzaki clan]]. Another branch of his lineage led to the establishment of the [[Ariake clan]], [[Ogihara clan]], and other families active in later eras. Through his daughter (or possibly his son Kanesuke's daughter) and her husband, [[Ban no Kanesada]], the [[Kimozuka clan]] and related houses emerged.
== Biography == Taira no Suemoto served as a senior official in the [[:en:Dazaifu|Dazaifu]] government, holding the position of [[Dazai Dainagon]] with a Rank equivalent to [[Jushichiino]]. This role allowed him substantial influence in Kyushu. During the [[Manju era]] (1024–1028), he and his brother [[Taira no Yoshimune]] developed an extensive estate centered in [[:en:Hyūga Province|Hyūga Province]]. Suemoto dedicated the newly developed [[Shima-tsu Manor]] to [[:en:Fujiwara no Yorimichi|Fujiwara no Yorimichi]], ensuring its protection under the powerful Fujiwara regency. In 1029, Suemoto engaged in a dispute involving the burning of Fujiwara Yoshitaka’s residence in [[:en:Ōsumi Province|Ōsumi Province]]. He avoided punishment by offering a significant tribute to [[Dazai no Daini]] Fujiwara no Koremune. Suemoto also played a key role in religious patronage, establishing [[Kamikura Shrine]], dedicated to [[Amaterasu]] and other deities, near what is now [[:en:Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki Prefecture]]. His shrine-based initiatives were central to the cultural and economic integration of Hyūga Province into the imperial domain.
== Tomb and Legacy == Taira no Suemoto's final residence and burial site, known as Hashino Gosho, is located in [[Soo City]], [[:en:Kagoshima Prefecture|Kagoshima Prefecture]]. His grave is preserved as a municipal cultural asset, and his spirit is venerated at [[Kami Hashira Shrine]] (神柱宮) in [[:en:Miyakonojo|Miyakonojo]]. A commemorative monument honoring his contributions stands at the modern Kamikura Shrine.
== Related Topics == * [[:en:Hyūga Province|Hyūga Province]] * [[Bandō Hachihei]] * [[Taira clan]]

revision.comment: Bot: add interwikis from Wikidata
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:平季基]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:平季基]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:平季基]] {{Infobox person|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11482381]] Wikidata name: Taira no Suemoto [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:42, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Cite-sources-needed|date=December 2022}} '''Taira no Suemoto''' (平 季基) was a regional government official during the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].
== Origin == Taira no Suemoto's lineage is unclear due to limited historical records. He is believed to be a member of the [[Kanmu Taira clan]] (桓武平氏), specifically of the [[Hanjō branch]] (繁盛流) connected to the [[Daijō clan]] and a cadet branch of the [[Iza clan]] of [[:en:Hitachi Province|Hitachi Province]]. This lineage includes prominent ancestors like [[Taira no Tamekane]], noted for his contributions during the [[:en:Toi invasion|Toi invasion]]. However, other accounts suggest Suemoto descended from the [[Bandō Hachihei]] (坂東八平氏), specifically the seventh-generation progeny of [[:en:Taira no Yoshifumi|Taira no Yoshifumi]], or that he belonged to the [[Satsuma Taira clan]] established by [[Taira no Sadatoki]]. Some scholars, such as Tokushige Asakichi, theorize that Taira no Suemoto was closely related to the descendants of [[Taira no Masasuke]] and [[Taira no Masayasu]], notable figures from the lineage of [[Taira no Takamune]]. What is certain is his role in the foundation and administration of [[Shima-tsu Manor]], one of Japan's largest estates.
=== Descendants === Suemoto's known descendants include [[Iza no Heiji Kanesuke]], who settled in [[:en:Hizen Province|Hizen Province]] and established the [[Kanzaki clan]]. Another branch of his lineage led to the establishment of the [[Ariake clan]], [[Ogihara clan]], and other families active in later eras. Through his daughter (or possibly his son Kanesuke's daughter) and her husband, [[Ban no Kanesada]], the [[Kimozuka clan]] and related houses emerged.
== Biography == Taira no Suemoto served as a senior official in the [[:en:Dazaifu|Dazaifu]] government, holding the position of [[Dazai Dainagon]] with a Rank equivalent to [[Jushichiino]]. This role allowed him substantial influence in Kyushu. During the [[Manju era]] (1024–1028), he and his brother [[Taira no Yoshimune]] developed an extensive estate centered in [[:en:Hyūga Province|Hyūga Province]]. Suemoto dedicated the newly developed [[Shima-tsu Manor]] to [[:en:Fujiwara no Yorimichi|Fujiwara no Yorimichi]], ensuring its protection under the powerful Fujiwara regency. In 1029, Suemoto engaged in a dispute involving the burning of Fujiwara Yoshitaka’s residence in [[:en:Ōsumi Province|Ōsumi Province]]. He avoided punishment by offering a significant tribute to [[Dazai no Daini]] Fujiwara no Koremune. Suemoto also played a key role in religious patronage, establishing [[Kamikura Shrine]], dedicated to [[Amaterasu]] and other deities, near what is now [[:en:Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki Prefecture]]. His shrine-based initiatives were central to the cultural and economic integration of Hyūga Province into the imperial domain.
== Tomb and Legacy == Taira no Suemoto's final residence and burial site, known as Hashino Gosho, is located in [[Soo City]], [[:en:Kagoshima Prefecture|Kagoshima Prefecture]]. His grave is preserved as a municipal cultural asset, and his spirit is venerated at [[Kami Hashira Shrine]] (神柱宮) in [[:en:Miyakonojo|Miyakonojo]]. A commemorative monument honoring his contributions stands at the modern Kamikura Shrine.
== Related Topics == * [[:en:Hyūga Province|Hyūga Province]] * [[Bandō Hachihei]] * [[Taira clan]]

== Origin == Taira no Suemoto's lineage is unclear due to limited historical records. He is believed to be a member of the [[Kanmu Taira clan]] (桓武平氏), specifically of the [[Hanjō branch]] (繁盛流) connected to the [[Daijō clan]] and a cadet branch of the [[Iza clan]] of {{ill|Hitachi Province|en|Hitachi Province|WD=Q1055353|lt=Hitachi Province}}. This lineage includes prominent ancestors like [[Taira no Tamekane]], noted for his contributions during the {{ill|Toi invasion|en|Toi invasion|WD=Q703655|lt=Toi invasion}}. However, other accounts suggest Suemoto descended from the [[Bandō Hachihei]] (坂東八平氏), specifically the seventh-generation progeny of {{ill|Taira no Yoshifumi|en|Taira no Yoshifumi|WD=Q11483528|lt=Taira no Yoshifumi}}, or that he belonged to the [[Satsuma Taira clan]] established by [[Taira no Sadatoki]]. Some scholars, such as Tokushige Asakichi, theorize that Taira no Suemoto was closely related to the descendants of [[Taira no Masasuke]] and [[Taira no Masayasu]], notable figures from the lineage of [[Taira no Takamune]]. What is certain is his role in the foundation and administration of [[Shima-tsu Manor]], one of Japan's largest estates.
=== Descendants === Suemoto's known descendants include [[Iza no Heiji Kanesuke]], who settled in {{ill|Hizen Province|en|Hizen Province|WD=Q906926|lt=Hizen Province}} and established the [[Kanzaki clan]]. Another branch of his lineage led to the establishment of the [[Ariake clan]], [[Ogihara clan]], and other families active in later eras. Through his daughter (or possibly his son Kanesuke's daughter) and her husband, [[Ban no Kanesada]], the [[Kimozuka clan]] and related houses emerged.
== Biography == Taira no Suemoto served as a senior official in the {{ill|Dazaifu|en|Dazaifu|WD=Q1179277|lt=Dazaifu}} government, holding the position of [[Dazai Dainagon]] with a Rank equivalent to [[Jushichiino]]. This role allowed him substantial influence in Kyushu. During the [[Manju era]] (1024–1028), he and his brother [[Taira no Yoshimune]] developed an extensive estate centered in {{ill|Hyūga Province|en|Hyūga Province|WD=Q513737|lt=Hyūga Province}}. Suemoto dedicated the newly developed [[Shima-tsu Manor]] to {{ill|Fujiwara no Yorimichi|en|Fujiwara no Yorimichi|WD=Q1370620|lt=Fujiwara no Yorimichi}}, ensuring its protection under the powerful Fujiwara regency. In 1029, Suemoto engaged in a dispute involving the burning of Fujiwara Yoshitaka’s residence in {{ill|Ōsumi Province|en|Ōsumi Province|WD=Q981002|lt=Ōsumi Province}}. He avoided punishment by offering a significant tribute to [[Dazai no Daini]] Fujiwara no Koremune. Suemoto also played a key role in religious patronage, establishing [[Kamikura Shrine]], dedicated to [[Amaterasu]] and other deities, near what is now {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|en|Miyazaki Prefecture|WD=Q130300|lt=Miyazaki Prefecture}}. His shrine-based initiatives were central to the cultural and economic integration of Hyūga Province into the imperial domain.
== Tomb and Legacy == Taira no Suemoto's final residence and burial site, known as Hashino Gosho, is located in [[Soo City]], {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|en|Kagoshima Prefecture|WD=Q15701|lt=Kagoshima Prefecture}}. His grave is preserved as a municipal cultural asset, and his spirit is venerated at [[Kami Hashira Shrine]] (神柱宮) in [[:en:Miyakonojo|Miyakonojo]]. A commemorative monument honoring his contributions stands at the modern Kamikura Shrine.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Hyūga Province|en|Hyūga Province|WD=Q513737|lt=Hyūga Province}} * [[Bandō Hachihei]] * [[Taira clan]]
*
* [[Kanbashira-gū]] * * [[Nyakuichi Ōji Shrine (So City)]] * [[Kanzaki Manor]] nu *

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:平季基]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:平季基]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:平季基]] {{Infobox person|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}}}} {{Cite-sources-needed|date=December 2022}} '''Taira no Suemoto''' (平 季基) was a regional government official during the late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}.
== Origin == Taira no Suemoto's lineage is unclear due to limited historical records. He is believed to be a member of the [[Kanmu Taira clan]] (桓武平氏), specifically of the [[Hanjō branch]] (繁盛流) connected to the [[Daijō clan]] and a cadet branch of the [[Iza clan]] of {{ill|Hitachi Province|en|Hitachi Province|WD=Q1055353|lt=Hitachi Province|1=Hitachi Province}}. This lineage includes prominent ancestors like [[Taira no Tamekane]], noted for his contributions during the {{ill|Toi invasion|en|Toi invasion|WD=Q703655|lt=Toi invasion|1=Toi invasion}}. However, other accounts suggest Suemoto descended from the [[Bandō Hachihei]] (坂東八平氏), specifically the seventh-generation progeny of {{ill|Taira no Yoshifumi|en|Taira no Yoshifumi|WD=Q11483528|lt=Taira no Yoshifumi|1=Taira no Yoshifumi}}, or that he belonged to the [[Satsuma Taira clan]] established by [[Taira no Sadatoki]]. Some scholars, such as Tokushige Asakichi, theorize that Taira no Suemoto was closely related to the descendants of [[Taira no Masasuke]] and [[Taira no Masayasu]], notable figures from the lineage of [[Taira no Takamune]]. What is certain is his role in the foundation and administration of [[Shima-tsu Manor]], one of Japan's largest estates.
=== Descendants === Suemoto's known descendants include [[Iza no Heiji Kanesuke]], who settled in {{ill|Hizen Province|en|Hizen Province|WD=Q906926|lt=Hizen Province|1=Hizen Province}} and established the [[Kanzaki clan]]. Another branch of his lineage led to the establishment of the [[Ariake clan]], [[Ogihara clan]], and other families active in later eras. Through his daughter (or possibly his son Kanesuke's daughter) and her husband, [[Ban no Kanesada]], the [[Kimozuka clan]] and related houses emerged.
== Biography == Taira no Suemoto served as a senior official in the {{ill|Dazaifu|en|Dazaifu|WD=Q1179277|lt=Dazaifu|1=Dazaifu}} government, holding the position of [[Dazai Dainagon]] with a Rank equivalent to [[Jushichiino]]. This role allowed him substantial influence in Kyushu. During the [[Manju era]] (1024–1028), he and his brother [[Taira no Yoshimune]] developed an extensive estate centered in {{ill|Hyūga Province|en|Hyūga Province|WD=Q513737|lt=Hyūga Province|1=Hyūga Province}}. Suemoto dedicated the newly developed [[Shima-tsu Manor]] to {{ill|Fujiwara no Yorimichi|en|Fujiwara no Yorimichi|WD=Q1370620|lt=Fujiwara no Yorimichi|1=Fujiwara no Yorimichi}}, ensuring its protection under the powerful Fujiwara regency. In 1029, Suemoto engaged in a dispute involving the burning of Fujiwara Yoshitaka’s residence in {{ill|Ōsumi Province|en|Ōsumi Province|WD=Q981002|lt=Ōsumi Province|1=Ōsumi Province}}. He avoided punishment by offering a significant tribute to [[Dazai no Daini]] Fujiwara no Koremune. Suemoto also played a key role in religious patronage, establishing [[Kamikura Shrine]], dedicated to [[Amaterasu]] and other deities, near what is now {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|en|Miyazaki Prefecture|WD=Q130300|lt=Miyazaki Prefecture|1=Miyazaki Prefecture}}. His shrine-based initiatives were central to the cultural and economic integration of Hyūga Province into the imperial domain.
== Tomb and Legacy == Taira no Suemoto's final residence and burial site, known as Hashino Gosho, is located in [[Soo City]], {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|en|Kagoshima Prefecture|WD=Q15701|lt=Kagoshima Prefecture|1=Kagoshima Prefecture}}. His grave is preserved as a municipal cultural asset, and his spirit is venerated at [[Kami Hashira Shrine]] (神柱宮) in [[:en:Miyakonojo|Miyakonojo]]. A commemorative monument honoring his contributions stands at the modern Kamikura Shrine.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Hyūga Province|en|Hyūga Province|WD=Q513737|lt=Hyūga Province|1=Hyūga Province}} * [[Bandō Hachihei]] * [[Taira clan]]
*
* [[Kanbashira-gū]] * * [[Nyakuichi Ōji Shrine (So City)]] * [[Kanzaki Manor]] nu *
